214131.transcribe: Good observation that a question that seems simple and maybe even solved can literally explode when you start to really get into it and hear different opinions. And more and more perspectives or facets of it become apparent. That's right. In technical spaces, we do think of intelligence as something very functional. And any other discussion about that's not so technical, for example, consciousness and soul and anything like that, we tend to very quickly reduce all that to technical or, I mean, to something functional as well, which I am personally bothered by.
214131.polish:
Good observation that a question that seems simple and maybe even "solved" (to some people) can literally explode when you start to really get into it and hear different opinions.

Yes, you're absolutely right -- in technical fields, we typically view intelligence in a functional context. And discussions that extend beyond technical aspects, such as those involving consciousness or the soul, are often quickly reduced to functional terms. I find this tendency concerning.

"I am not saying sentience is required for intelligence, but I do think the discussion made me realize how often we slide between these categories without really realizing that we're doing so." -- Absolutely! Great point!

241621.transcribe: Aha, you're bringing up consciousness as a requirement for thinking. That's interesting. There's no way to refute that with any proof. Of course, we don't know exactly what consciousness is and what its role is in intelligence or in thinking. But of course, there are counter arguments that can be given for thinking and consciousness. But yeah, definitely an interesting point. Yep. Simple innovation is definitely something we can expect from AI in 5, 10, and 100 years. I don't know if it's going to take a hundred years to conquer cancer. My hope is it'll take much less time than that. Let's hope in the next 5 to 10 years. Programmers, 5 to 10 years. I don't know. It seems like programming is going to go away. Programming is going to go away or should go away in the next two years. But yeah, maybe you're right because programmers will stick around because there's so much code already written, legacy code that needs to be maintained. Yeah, good question about what's stopping AI from developing self-consciousness. Thank you.

287757.transcribe: That is an interesting view of free will. I first thought you were referring to free will as just being the possibility, just being the knowledge of the possibility to do something. And I actually like that definition a lot. I'm not going to call that a definition, but I like that notion a lot. But then you extended that to the knowledge of dolphins being able to use sonar. And that's very interesting. I wouldn't, I myself never thought of free will as having knowledge like that. But that's definitely something to think about. You're absolutely right. We did not talk about intelligence as a spectrum, which I usually do, but that I guess we didn't have a chance to do that. But yes, there are actually several things to say about intelligence being a spectrum and I'm going to try and bring this up in class at some point. Because it is definitely not an on and off or a binary thing, or at least doesn't seem like it is. I agree with you that intelligence systems seem to have parts, but I don't know if that's enough to say that they have to have parts, or whether intelligence itself requires parts. Maybe an intelligence system does, or maybe it develops that due to some, you know, environmental need. But does that answer what intelligence should be, or not what intelligence should be, but what intelligence it is, or what intelligence is? A very good question about what are the optimization functions in our head.
287757.polish:
You presented an interesting perspective on free will. Initially, I interpreted your view as relating to the knowledge of the mere possibility of action, which I find appealing. While I hesitate to label it a definition, I appreciate that notion.

You then extended your thoughts to the knowledge of dolphins using sonar, which I had not previously considered in relation to free will. This is definitely a thought-provoking connection, and I find it intriguing to think about how knowledge can influence our understanding of free will.

I recognize that we did not discuss intelligence as a spectrum, a topic I typically address. I plan to incorporate this discussion into our class at some point, as intelligence does not seem to be a binary concept. There are several facets to explore regarding intelligence as a spectrum, and I believe it is essential to acknowledge that it is not simply an on-and-off or binary phenomenon.

I agree that intelligent systems appear to have components, but I am uncertain if this necessitates that intelligence itself requires parts. It is possible that an intelligent system develops these components in response to environmental needs. This raises important questions about the nature of intelligence. I think it's a great question that you are asking about what the optimization functions are in our minds.

288357.transcribe:
I think that's a fantastic observation that you made about the difference between why a group of people don't seem intelligent, but a group of ants do or a group of neurons do or a group of ants or neurons does because yes, a group of ants and a group of neurons are behaving in similar ways that they are being very efficient and doing only the simplest jobs But human beings are more complex and their communications are complex. So when multiple human beings work together they're they're more sophisticated slash complex thinking and Communication is getting in the way Absolutely fantastic observation on your part.

Also, I would generally agree with you that the gap between human intelligence and AI is probably not as huge as most people think it is.
288357.polish:
I think that's a fantastic observation that you made about the difference between why a group of people may not seem intelligent while a group of ants or a group of neurons does. You noted that groups of ants and neurons exhibit efficient behavior through simple tasks, which is true. However, human beings are inherently more complex, and this complexity in our communication and thought processes can sometimes hinder effective collaboration. Your ability to recognize this distinction is commendable and reflects a deep understanding of the dynamics at play. Absolutely fantastic observation on your part.

Additionally, I would generally agree with you that the gap between human intelligence and AI is probably not as vast as many people believe it to be.

290812.transcribe: Totally appreciate your disagreement with the idea of selfishness. One thing that I think I said in class, but I know I said in class but I should have stressed on more is that the whole notion of selfishness is at the micro level. That is to say it is at the neuron level or even lower than that, not just the neuron level. But even at the neuron level, you could say the neurons are just optimizing for some utility. And at that point, there's no point even saying those are selfish. All I'm saying is that it is grounded in what humans would call selfishness. But that at the macro level, of course, we are not selfish. We definitely act out of chivalry and love, and these are not selfishness. But when we see the underpinnings of what results in those macro effects, my claim is that the very lower level operations are basically akin to selfishness. Not that neurons are selfish. They cannot be selfish or not selfish. They just do what they're doing, and at the macro level, we perceive that as being unselfish. Very good observation about us not having to wait for evolutionary mutations to survive a changing environment. We can learn on the spot very quickly. We don't need to wait for millions of years or a very long time for some evolution to change our intelligence or at least our learning. So that's definitely a good point. A very good question about consciousness, the thing you said about wondering whether the line is actually between biological instinct and real intelligence, because yes, you're absolutely right. When we think about consciousness, we always refer to it in terms of some animal. It's our consciousness or a cow's consciousness or something like that. But what in the world is consciousness regardless of animal? Very good question.
290812.polish:
I appreciate your disagreement with the idea of selfishness. One point I intended to emphasize in class is that the notion of "selfishness" operates at a micro level, specifically at the neuron level and even lower. At this level, neurons are simply optimizing for some utility, and it is not quite meaningful to even label these processes as selfish. While I argue that these mechanisms at the micro level (the level of neurons and even lower) are grounded in what humans would call selfishness, at the macro level, we do not act selfishly. We often act out of chivalry and love, which are not selfish motivations. It's the underlying mechanism that seems to be doing only one thing, which is optimizing some utility. As suchh, neurons themselves cannot be classified as selfish or unselfish; they simply perform their functions. At the macro level, the resulting actions on our part certainly can be perceived as unselfish, which we are certainly capable of being.

You made an excellent observation regarding our ability to adapt to changing environments without waiting for evolutionary mutations. We can learn quickly and do not need to rely on millions of years of evolution to enhance our intelligence or learning capabilities. 

Your question about consciousness is also very insightful. You raised an important point about the distinction between biological instinct and real intelligence. When we discuss consciousness, we do often (always?) reference it in relation to specific animals, such as our own consciousness or that of a cow. However, the fundamental nature of consciousness, regardless of the species, remains a profound and complex question.
